title: How Much of Your Portfolio Should Be in Non‑U.S. Stocks?
author: Sigma Capital
metaDescription: For most long-term investors, international stocks should make up 5% to 40% of their portfolio, balancing diversification and risk. This range helps achieve bet
excerpt: For most long-term investors, international stocks should make up 5% to 40% of their portfolio, balancing diversification and risk. This range helps achieve better returns and mitigates U.S. market risks. Consult financial advisors for personalized advice.

content.introduction: For most long-term investors, international stocks should make up 5% to 40% of their portfolio, balancing diversification and risk. This range helps achieve better returns and mitigates U.S. market risks. Consult financial advisors for personalized advice.
body: J.P. Morgan Asset Management’s Long-Term Capital Market Assumptions suggest developed international stocks may produce better annual returns than U.S. equities over the next 10 to 15 years. The MSCI World Index, a common benchmark for global stocks, is still about 70% U.S. Adding just a 30% allocation to non-U.S. developed markets brings more diversification than it did a decade ago, when the U.S. and international split was closer to even. The U.S. stock market remains a major driver of global returns, but expectations are changing. Any forecasts, figures, opinions or investment techniques and strategies set out are for information purposes only, based on certain assumptions and current market conditions and are subject to change without prior notice. This material is for informational purposes only, and may inform you of certain products and services offered by J.P. Morgan’s wealth management businesses, part of JPMorgan Chase & Co. Products and services described, as well as associated fees, charges and interest rates, are subject to change in accordance with the applicable account agreements and may differ among geographic locations.
Experts suggest diversification with international stocks; they recommend a 35%-40% weighting in global markets for individuals. Global index
Focusing only on the 60% equity sleeve, we formed 21 different equity allocation mixes increasing/decreasing the international/US equity mix by 3% increments,
